input.background: A manufacturer has observed that over the course of 10 months, the numbers of products demanded were $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_{10}$, respectively. If we assume that these are i.i.d. observations of demand.
input.question:
Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{\mathrm{n}}$ and $X$ be random variables on a probability space. We say that $X_{\mathrm{n}}$ converges in probability to $X$ if
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} P\left(\left|X_n-X\right|<\varepsilon\right)=1 \quad \text { for every } \varepsilon>0\\
&\text { Similarly, } X_{\mathrm{n}} \text { converges with probability } 1 \text { (or almost surely) to } X \text { if }\\
&P\left(\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} X_n=X\right)=1 .
\end{aligned}
$$

output.method: 概率空间相关（如状态数）
output.answer: The definitions of convergence in probability and almost sure convergence are provided in the question.

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 1: Basic Probability, Statistics, and Reliability
